coredata.dc:title: One-step electrodeposition of carbon quantum dots and transition metal ions for N-doped carbon coupled with NiFe oxide clusters: A high-performance electrocatalyst for oxygen evolution
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Shaoxuan
Du, Ruoqiu
Yu, Yihuan
Zhang, Zhengping
Wang, Feng
coredata.dc:description: NiFe-based materials are excellent electrocatalysts for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) to promise as replacements for Ru- and Ir-based anode materials for water splitting. In this work, NiFeOx clusters strongly coupled N-doped carbon (NiFeOx©NC) hybrid is prepared by a one-step electrodeposition process in an aqueous solution containing Ni2+, Fe3+, and carbon quantum dots. The highly coupled interface between the sub-nano NiFeOx clusters and N-doped carbon results in the enhanced OER performance superior to the commercial dimensionally stable anode (RuIr-based), as evidenced by its lower overpotential (195 mV at 10 mA cm−2) and faster kinetics (Tafel slope of 33 mV dec−1), along with the excellent long-term durability, rate capability and environmental adaptability. It is believed that the NiFeOx©NC hybrid and electrodeposition–electrocatalysis strategy developed in this work provide new possibilities for the development of clean electrochemical technology.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NiFeOx clusters
Carbon quantum dots
N-doped carbons
Heterogeneous catalysts
Oxygen evolution reaction

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Illustration of ① the one-step cathodic electrodeposition process for preparing the NiFeOx©NC hybrid ② directly as the OER-active anode for water splitting. (b) SEM image of NiFeOx©NC. (c) HAADF-STEM image of NiFeOx©NC. Inset: corresponding particle size distribution of the NiFeOx nanoparticles. (d) Atomic-resolution HAADF-STEM image of NiFeOx©NC. (e) HAADF-STEM and corresponding EDX mapping images of NiFeOx©NC.

High-resolution XPS spectra of (a) C 1s, (b) Ni 2p and (c) Fe 2p for NiFeOx©NC and NiFeOx. (d) C K-edge XANES spectra of graphite sheet, CQDs and NiFeOx©NC. (e) Ni K-edge and (f) Fe K-edge XANES spectra of NiFeOx©NC and NiFeOx.

(a) IR-compensated LSV curves of NiOx, FeOx, NiFeOx, NiOx©NC, FeOx©NC and NiFeOx©NC in O2-saturated 1 M KOH at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1. (b) Tafel plots derived from the corresponding data in (a). A comparison between the present work and that in the literature for (c) kinetics (Tafel slope), activity (the overpotential required to achieve 10 mA cm−2), and (d) TOF.

(a) IR-compensated LSV curves of Graphite sheet, RuO2, DSA and NiFeOx©NC in O2-saturated 1 M KOH at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1. (b) Chronopotentiometric curves of DSA and NiFeOx©NC in 1 M KOH solution with current densities of 5 mA cm−2, 10 mA cm−2, 20 mA cm−2, 50 mA cm−2 and 10 mA cm−2 for each 2 h in sequence. (c) Long-term chronopotentiometric curves of NiFeOx©NC in 1 M KOH solution with current densities of 20 mA cm−2, 50 mA cm−2, –10 mA cm−2 and 50 mA cm−2 in sequence. Inset: photograph of the three-electrode system after chronoamperometry at 20 mA cm−2 for ca. 8.6 h. (d) The cell voltage in different electrolytes during overall water splitting reaction in a two-electrode configuration (with NiFeOx©NC as anode and De Nora activated cathode (AC) as cathode). (e) Photograph of the overall water splitting reaction in a two-electrode configuration driven by a 1.5 V AA battery.

head.ce:title: One-step electrodeposition of carbon quantum dots and transition metal ions for N-doped carbon coupled with NiFe oxide clusters: A high-performance electrocatalyst for oxygen evolution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering
Beijing Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Process and Technology for Materials

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Process and Technology for Materials, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing, 100029, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering
Beijing Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Process and Technology for Materials

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: NiFe-based materials are excellent electrocatalysts for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) to promise as replacements for Ru- and Ir-based anode materials for water splitting. In this work, NiFeOx clusters strongly coupled N-doped carbon (NiFeOx©NC) hybrid is prepared by a one-step electrodeposition process in an aqueous solution containing Ni2+, Fe3+, and carbon quantum dots. The highly coupled interface between the sub-nano NiFeOx clusters and N-doped carbon results in the enhanced OER performance superior to the commercial dimensionally stable anode (RuIr-based), as evidenced by its lower overpotential (195 mV at 10 mA cm−2) and faster kinetics (Tafel slope of 33 mV dec−1), along with the excellent long-term durability, rate capability and environmental adaptability. It is believed that the NiFeOx©NC hybrid and electrodeposition–electrocatalysis strategy developed in this work provide new possibilities for the development of clean electrochemical technology.

An enhanced NiFe-based oxygen evolution reaction (OER) electrocatalysis is proposed for preparation of NiFeOx clusters highly coupled with N-doped carbon (NiFeOx©NC) hybrid via a one-step electrodeposition.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Boosting the sluggish kinetics of the anodic oxygen evolution reaction (OER) is a crucial step in the development of several electrochemical devices (e.g., water-splitting electrolyzers and rechargeable metal-air batteries) capable of generating clean and sustainable energy [1–3]. The efficiency of OER is limited by its four-electron transfer pathway and highly positive anodic potential, where most metals generally show high overpotential and electrochemical instability [4,5]. Although oxides of Ru and Ir incorporated in a dimensionally stable anode (DSA) have been employed as commercial anodes for almost 50 years, the high cost and scarcity of these precious metals still hinder the large-scale application of such devices for practical energy conversion and storage [6,7].

Ni-based and NiFe-based compounds (e.g., oxides [8,9], hydroxides [10,11], and phosphides [12,13]) are considered to be the most cost-effective alternatives as anode materials, since their active surface states (i.e., Ni3+) afford OER activities close to Ru- and Ir-based materials. The synthesis of effective Ni-based OER electrocatalysts depends crucially on the support or substrate, since this facilitates both particle dispersion and electron conduction [10,14,15]. In addition, the extensive electronic effects arising from supports such as N-doped carbon may lead to interfacial modulation of the resulting hybrid, which can modify the surface charge state of Ni-based particles and enhance their OER catalytic activity [15]. However, such heterogeneous or composite catalysts synthesized by traditional processes (utilizing nano or micro supports, or via high-temperature pyrolysis) generally show very weak interfacial interactions as a result of the limited contact area between “large” particles and “large” supports [10,16]. In an attempt to enhance the interfacial interaction, we proposed a novel strategy to prepare the strongly coupled NiFeOx clusters and N-doped carbon (NiFeOx©NC) hybrid, derived from metal ions and sub-nano carbon quantum dots. The highly coupled interface of the sub-nano NiFeOx clusters on successive N-doped carbon result in enhanced performance towards OER. The NiFeOx©NC hybrid present an ultra-low overpotential with a fast kinetics as well as excellent long-term durability in a series of practical operations.

As shown in Fig. 1 a, the synthesis of NiFeOx©NC involved one-step electrodeposition (at a constant potential of −1.0 V versus a saturated calomel electrode) on graphite sheets in a standard three-electrode electrochemical system containing Ni2+, Fe3+ and N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs). The sub-nano N-CQDs (with an average diameter of ca. 3.8 nm, Fig. S1) were prepared by a microwave-assisted method (seeing the Experimental Section for details). When compared with N-CQDs (−19.2 mV), NiFe/CQDs showed positive-shift in Zeta potential (9.0 mV) (Fig. S2 and Table S1). In ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra (Fig. S3), the absorption peak (at 346 nm) of N-CQDs obviously appeared in the spectrum of NiFe/CQDs. These results proved that the Ni2+ and Fe3+ ions had been successfully coated on N-CQDs by electrostatic interaction, which facilitated the subsequent cathodic co-electrodeposition process. When the deposition potential was applied, the NO3 − around the working electrode (graphite sheet) were reduced to produce hydroxide ions and then reacted with metal ions. Due to the electrostatic interaction between Ni2+, Fe3+ ions and N-CQDs, the N-CQDs would be easily deposited along with bimetallic moieties and form a carbon substrate on the electrode surface. We optimized bath contents and deposition time (Figs. S4–S5). The NiFeOx©NC hybrid obtained by the electrodeposition for 60 min with the N-CQD concentration of 0.32 g L−1 exhibited the best OER electrocatalytic activity, and this NiFeOx©NC materials were used for further analysis as discussed below.

After electrodeposition on graphite sheets, NiFeOx©NC showed a rough morphology, whereas NiFeOx (using the same electrodeposition process of Ni2+ and Fe3+ ions on graphite sheets, but without N-CQDs) afforded a material with a layered texture (Fig. 1b and Fig. S6). Static water contact angle measurements (Fig. S7) showed that the NiFeOx©NC had high hydrophilicity (with a water contact angle of 5.2°), which should facilitate the fast removal of O2 bubbles as they form in the OER reaction [17,18]. Aberration corrected high-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) afforded further insight into the internal structure of the NiFeOx©NC hybrid. As shown in Fig. 1c, numerous individual small metal clusters (with an average diameter of ca. 1.9 nm, Fig. 1c inset) were homogeneously distributed on the carbon support. The atomic resolution HAADF-STEM image (Fig. 1d) and energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) mapping analysis (Fig. 1e) showed that the NiFeOx metal clusters were composed of dozens of Ni, Fe and O atoms. Notably, an amorphous carbonaceous structure was observed around these NiFeOx clusters (Fig. 1c and d), which is quite different from the graphite sheet with obvious graphitic layers (Fig. S8). It indicated that the N-CQDs could act as nitrogen-containing carbonaceous bricks to build up N-doped carbon moieties, which separated the NiFeOx clusters. The Fourier transform infrared spectrometer and Raman spectra (Fig. S9) indicated that the Ni/Fe species in NiFeOx and NiFeOx©NC exist in the form of layered double hydroxide and metallic oxides, respectively. And the difference between NiFeOx and NiFeOx©NC, especially for the size (200–300 nm for NiFeOx estimated from SEM), inevitably result in some specific characteristics, where NiFeOx©NC exhibited obvious red-shift compared to NiFeOx in UV–vis spectra (Fig. S10). In addition, due to the small size of NiFeOx clusters, the large contact area between the NiFeOx clusters and N-doped carbon should provide continuous electron transport and a chemical environment advantageous for OER electrocatalysis [19].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to investigate the surface chemical composition and electronic structure of NiFeOx and NiFeOx©NC. The XPS survey spectra (Fig. S11) confirmed the presence of Ni (3.49 at.%), Fe (1.86 at.%), O (33.73 at.%), C (54.98 at.%) and N (5.94 at.%) in NiFeOx©NC (Table S2). Due to the introduction of N-CQDs during the synthesis, the NiFeOx©NC exhibited higher contents of functional C moieties (C–O/C–N at 285.7 eV, CO at 286.7 eV and O–CO at 288.9 eV) and additional N moieties (metal–N at 398.7 eV, pyrrolic N at 399.8 eV, graphitic N at 401.2 eV and oxidized N at 402.4 eV) compared with the NiFeOx sample (Fig. 2 a and Fig. S12) [20,21]. The high-resolution Ni 2p XPS spectra for NiFeOx and NiFeOx©NC were deconvoluted into two spin-orbit doublets and two shakeup satellites, corresponding to the Ni(II) and Ni(III) species, respectively (Fig. 2b). Meanwhile, the major peak for NiFeOx©NC can be attributed to Ni(III) (69.0%) rather than Ni(II) (31.0%), which was significantly different from NiFeOx (46.7% Ni(III) and 53.3% Ni(II), Table S3) [9,23]. The predominance of the high-valence state was also observed in the Fe 2p region. As shown in Fig. 2c, the NiFeOx©NC sample showed a higher relative content of Fe(III) species (60.4%) than NiFeOx (42.2% Fe(III)).

To further analyze the electronic structure of NiFeOx©NC, X-ray absorption near edge structure (XANES) measurements were carried out. The C K-edge spectra (Fig. 2d) showed that the NiFeOx©NC hybrid was composed of a relatively disordered carbonaceous species, where presented in lower π*C
C (ca. 285.8 eV), σ*C – C (ca. 292.3 eV) contents and a higher π*C – N/C – O (ca. 288.5 eV) content in comparison with the graphite sheet. In addition, NiFeOx©NC also showed weaker π*C
C peak and a stronger π*C – N/C – O peak than N-CQDs. These changes suggest a strong chemical interaction occurs between NiFeOx clusters and N-doped carbon, corresponding to the large amount of metal–N–C and/or metal–O–C species [10,24], which is consistent with the XPS results (Figs. S12 and S13). Moreover, the reduced intensity of the π*C
C transition revealed charge transfer from metal ions into the carbon of the honeycomb lattice bridged by N and/or O [24]. Fig. 2e showed that NiFeOx©NC exhibited a similar Ni K-edge XANES curve to NiFeOx, but a more positively charged state with a high-energy-shifted (by ca. 0.4 eV) edge and an increased white-line peak. Analogously, in the Fe K-edge XANES spectra (Fig. 2f), more positively charged states of Fe were observed in NiFeOx©NC when compared with NiFeOx, consistent with the XPS results. These results all indicated that a strong charge transfer exists between the NiFeOx clusters and N-doped carbon, which should lead to enhanced interfacial interactions. The effect of any such interactions on the efficacy of the NiFeOx©NC hybrid in OER electrocatalysis was therefore investigated in detail.

The OER electrocatalytic activity of NiFeOx©NC was performed in O2-saturated 1 M KOH solution with a standard three-electrode system. IR-compensation electrochemical measurements for all the as-prepared electrodes were normalized by the geometric surface area of electrode (1 cm × 1 cm, the same below). Due to the content of Fe has a significant effect on the OER activity, the atomic ratio of Ni to Fe was optimized (Fig. S14). It is obvious that the OER activity is not monotonically enhanced with the increasing Fe content. This result is consistent with the reported literatures, owing to the higher activity of NiFeOOH species (formed at low content of Fe) than that of NiFe2O4 (formed at high content of Fe) [9,22,25]. As a result, Ni69Fe31Ox©NC (the Ni/Fe ratio obtained by ICP-MS, Table S4) exhibited the best electrocatalytic performance, hence only the Ni69Fe31Ox©NC (denoted as NiFeOx©NC for the sake of simplicity) was used for subsequent analysis (Fig. S14). Moreover, the effect of the interfacial interactions on the OER activity was investigated (Fig. 3 a). Compared with NiFeOx (overpotential, η of 243 mV), NiFeOx©NC presented similar electrochemical surface area (Figs. S15 and S16) and approximate thickness (Fig. S17), but performed better OER activity with η of 195 mV (an anodic potential of 1.425 V vs. the reversible hydrogen electrode, RHE) when delivering a current density of 10 mA cm−2. Considering the sluggish OER activity on the N-doped carbon (NC, Fig. S18), the enhanced performance of the NiFeOx©NC is proposed to be contributed by the strong interactions between NiFeOx clusters and NC substrate. Analogously, when compared with NiOx and FeOx (prepared by electrodeposition of Ni2+ or Fe3+ ions on graphite sheet without N-CQDs), the corresponding hybrids (NiOx©NC and FeOx©NC) both showed enhanced OER activity. (NiOx©NC: 271 mV; FeOx©NC: 388 mV; NiOx: 339 mV; FeOx: 410 mV). In addition, the NiFeOx©NC also possessed the fastest OER kinetics process with a smaller Tafel slope (33 mV dec−1, Fig. 3b) than NiFeOx (36 mV dec−1), NiOx©NC (104 mV dec−1), FeOx©NC (59 mV dec−1), NiOx (106 mV dec−1 mV) and FeOx (65 mV dec−1). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy analysis was also performed to evaluate the kinetics of interfacial processes (Fig. S19). The charge transfer resistance value of NiFeOx©NC (5.7 Ω cm2) was much smaller than those of the other materials, demonstrating its higher transfer coefficient for OER kinetics. Indeed, the NiFeOx©NC hybrid exhibited state-of-the-art OER properties with either a lower overpotential or faster kinetics than other previously reported Ni-based materials (Fig. 3c; the detailed parameters are listed in Table S5) [11,22,26–38].

To obtain an insight into the origin of the intrinsic activity or efficacy of utilization of metal atoms, the turnover frequency (TOF) was estimated for the Ni and Fe atoms in NiFeOx©NC. It was assumed that each metal atom in NiFeOx©NC participated in the OER catalytic reaction, and the estimated TOF was thus the lower limit. NiFeOx©NC exhibited a higher TOF value (0.52 s−1 at η = 350 mV) than either NiFeOx (0.23 s−1 at η = 350 mV) or recently reported OER electrocatalysts, including some precious metal (Ru and Ir)-based materials (Fig. 3d; detailed values are listed in Table S6) [4,22,29,30,32–34,39–41], confirming that the highly-coupled NiFeOx clusters in NiFeOx©NC can provide excellent OER activity. To further investigate the origin of the enhanced activity, we performed Hall effect test. Compared with NiFeOx, NiFeOx©NC exhibited much higher n-type carrier concentration (Table S7). Mott–Schottky electrochemical analysis (Fig. S20) also achieved the similar results, where the smaller slopes of the Mott–Schottky plots demonstrated that NiFeOx©NC had a much higher n-type carrier density [42,43]. The significantly enlarged donor density in NiFeOx©NC indicated that electrons can readily flow from NiFeOx clusters to NC. The charge delocalization on the NC/NiFeOx interface results in pulling down the valence band on the side of NiFeOx, leading to the improved OER performance of the NiFeOx©NC hybrid [15,44,45].

We also compared the performance of the NiFeOx©NC hybrid with that of a commercial dimensionally stable anode (DSA, consisting of Ru- and Ir-oxides on titanium mesh) and commercial RuO2 particles. The LSV curves in Fig. 4 a, along with the Tafel slope in Fig. S21, confirmed that the NiFeOx©NC hybrid offered a significant advantage over both DSA (η: 272 mV, Tafel slope: 76 mV dec−1) and RuO2 (η: 351 mV, Tafel slope: 82 mV dec−1). To assess the OER performance of NiFeOx©NC in practical applications, the electrolysis was conducted at different current densities for up to 10 h. As shown in Fig. 4b, the NiFeOx©NC showed much lower and more stable OER overpotential than the commercial DSA (the differences being 112, 155, 216, 400 and 166 mV at current densities of 5, 10, 20, 50 and 10 mA cm−2, respectively). Notably, even after the current density was reduced from 50 mA cm−2 back to 10 mA cm−2, the reversible overpotential of the NiFeOx©NC electrode recovered to a value (251 mV) similar to the previous overpotential (247 mV) at the same current density. Chronopotentiometric curves further confirmed the excellent durability of the electrode for long-term practical operation (Fig. 4c and Supplementary Movie 1). The NiFeOx©NC electrode exhibited excellent durability after continuous operation (at current densities of 20 and 50 mA cm−2) over 15 h; it also maintained a good OER performance after operating at reverse current (−10 mA cm−2), which simulated the switching of a practical electrolyzer between startup and shutdown. The excellent rate capability and durability of NiFeOx©NC can be attributed to two advantageous features of this material: one is the low overpotential (i.e., the low anodic potential), which prevents oxidation of the electrode; the other is its good corrosion resistance, which increases the endurance of the catalytic layer and substrate (Fig. S22). In addition, the experimentally detected of amount O2 was very close to the theoretical amount, showing that NiFeOx©NC gave nearly 100% Faradaic yield during the OER process (Fig. S23).
Supplementary video related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.nanoen.2020.105057

The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Multimedia component 1

In order to assess its efficacy under realistic operating conditions, the NiFeOx©NC hybrid was employed as the anode in different concentrations of a variety of electrolytes during overall water-splitting reaction in a two-electrode configuration (using an anode–cathode distance of 2 cm) with a commercially available active cathode (consisting of Ru oxides on nickel mesh). Fig. 4d showed that the NiFeOx©NC electrode could be used as an efficient OER electrode under a variety of operating conditions, especially in the case of concentrated alkaline electrolytes. In view of the ultralow cell voltage of the above NiFeOx©NC-based electrolyzer, we also employed this water-splitting device powered by a single-cell AA battery with a nominal voltage of ca. 1.5 V (Fig. 4e and Supplementary Movie 2). The abundant bubbles produced from the electrode confirmed the excellent OER performance of the NiFeOx©NC electrode.
Supplementary video related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.nanoen.2020.105057

The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Multimedia component 2 Multimedia component 2

In summary, a highly coupled hybrid has been fabricated using sub-nano NiFeOx clusters and N-doped carbon via a one-step electrodeposition of metal nitrate salts and N-CQDs. The N-CQDs act as nitrogen-containing carbonaceous bricks to build up successive layers of N-doped carbon which couple with the NiFeOx clusters. Due to the large and highly coupled interface between NiFeOx clusters and N-doped carbon, the NiFeOx©NC hybrid showed superior OER performance to commercial RuO2 catalyst and DSA electrode, as evidenced by its lower overpotential and faster kinetics, along with excellent long-term durability, rate capability and environmental adaptability under practical operating conditions. Therefore, we believe that this work not only opens up a facile approach to prepare high-efficiency anodes for water splitting devices, but also provides new possibilities for the development of cost-effective materials for other clean electrochemical technologies.

Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs). Typically, 2 g citric acid and 1 mL ethylenediamine was dissolved in 20 mL deionized (DI) water under ultrasonication 20 min to form a uniform solution. The mixture was then heated 5 min at 200 °C using a microwave digestion system (CEM Mars6). After cooled to room temperature, the product containing water-soluble N-GQDs was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to remove insoluble carbon product, and further dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 500 Da) for 2 days to remove unfused small molecules.

Synthesis of NiFeO
x
©NC. Graphite sheet (3 cm × 1 cm × 1 mm (t)) was sonicated in ethanol and water to remove the impurity, and then treated by annealing in air at 800 °C for 10 min to make it hydrophilic. The electrodeposition was carried out in a standard three-electrode electrochemical cell containing graphite sheet as the working electrode, a parallel positioned platinum plate as the counter electrode and a potassium chloride (KCl) saturated calomel as the reference electrode. The electrolyte bath contained 3 mM Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, 3 mM Fe(NO3)3·9H2O and a certain amount of N-CQDs. The optimized content of N-CQDs in electrolyte bath was 0.32 g L−1. The constant potential electrodeposition was then carried out at −1.0 V (vs. SCE) at 25 °C. The optimized deposition time has been determined to 60 min. To optimize the composition of NiFeOx©NC, the total mole of Ni2+ and Fe3+ in deposition solution was 6.0 mM while the ratio of Ni2+ and Fe3+ was varied systematically. After deposition, the graphite sheet was carefully withdrawn from the electrolyte, rinsed with water and ethanol, then sonicated briefly in ethanol and left dry in air. Besides NiFeOx©NC, NiOx©NC and FeOx©NC were also prepared following the same procedures in the absence of Fe3+ and Ni2+, respectively. For comparison, NiFeOx, NiOx, and FeOx were synthesized using electrolyte bath severally canting Ni2+Fe3+, Ni2+, Fe3+ as following the same procedures in the absence of N-CQDs. N-doped carbon (NC) was obtained by acid washing of NiFeOx©NC. The mass loading of active moiety was determined by mass difference before and after electrodeposition, which is ca. 0.46 mg cm−2 for NiFeOx©NC and 0.4 mg cm−2 for NiFeOx.

Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, JSM-6701/JEOL), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2010) and aberration-corrected scanning transmission electron microscope (STEM, JEMARM200F/JEOL) equipped with a spherical aberration corrector were used to observe the morphologic and structural characteristics of the samples. The Fourier Transform InfraRed (FT-IR) analysis was performed on a Nicolet 8700/Continuum XL with wavenumber from 2200 to 500 cm−1. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectroscopy was carried out on a Shimadzu UV-2450 with wavelength from 300 to 900 nm. Raman spectra were record on a Horiba Jobin Yvon LabRam HR800 with an excitation wavelength of 633 nm. The charge state distribution of samples was characterized by Zetasizer Nano ZS 90 (Malvern Instruments Ltd.). Chemical compositions and elemental oxidation states of the samples were investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific ESCALAB 250 with monochromatic Al Kα 150W X-ray sources and spot size of 500 μm, the pass energy is 200 eV for survey and 30 eV for high resolution scans). The elemental spectra were all corrected with respect to C1s peaks at 285 eV. The C K-edge XAFS spectra and Fe/Co K-edge XAFS spectra were severally acquired at beamline 4B7B and 1W1B station of the Beijing Synchrotron Radiation Facility, China. The Fe/Co K-edge XANES data were recorded in a fluorescence mode using a Lytle detector. The storage ring was working at the energy of 2.5 GeV with an average electron current of 250 mA. The hard Xray was monochromatized with Si (111) double-crystals. All samples were pelletized as disks of 13 mm diameter with 1 mm thickness using graphite powder as a binder. The acquired EXAFS data were processed according to the standard procedures using the ATHENA module implemented in the IFEFFIT software packages.

The electrochemical tests were carried out in a standard three-electrode system on a CHI760E electrochemical workstation ((CH Instruments Company, Austin, USA)). A platinum plate was utilized as the counter electrode. The potential was recorded using KCl saturated calomel as the reference electrode. All the potentials were calibrated to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) according to Nernst equation. Current density was calculated based on the geometric area of electrode (1 cm × 1 cm). All samples were first conducted the cyclic voltammetry (CV) test to get a stable curve at 50 mV s−1. The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) tests were conducted at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 for the polarization curves. All polarization curves were compensated with 90% iR-compensation. The electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were carried out at the 1.50 V in a frequency from 100 kHz to 0.01 Hz with a 5 mV amplitude. Chronopotentiometry was carried out under different current density in a standard three-electrode system to evaluate the electrode durability and anti-reverse current impact capability. The cell voltage was recorded in a two-electrode configuration to measure the catalytic performance in various concentrations of electrolytes.

The turnover frequency (TOF) of catalysts according to the equation:
T O F =
I
4 F n
Where I is the current (A) during the LSV measurement in 1 M KOH, F is the Faraday constant (a value of 96485 C/mol), and n is the number of the active sites (mol).

Shaoxuan Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Ruoqiu Du: Investigation, Data curation, Writing - review & editing. Yihuan Yu: Data curation, Writing - review & editing. Zhengping Zhang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Feng Wang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51432003, 51802011, and 51125007); the National Key R&D Program of China (2018YFB0105500); the Start-Up Fund for Talent Introduction of Beijing University of Chemical Technology (buctrc201806); the Fund of High-Performance Carbon-Based Electrodes for Energy Storage; and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (JD1906). The authors are grateful to the 1W1B and 4B7B station at Beijing Synchrotron Radiation Facility (BSRF) for the XAFS tests.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 3 Multimedia component 3

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.nanoen.2020.105057.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Shaoxuan Yang received his B.S. from Beijing University of Chemical Technology in 2015, and continued his studies as a Ph.D. candidate under the supervision of Prof. Feng Wang at the College of Materials Science and Engineering at Beijing University of Chemical Technology. His current research interests focus on the development of highly efficient electrocatalysts and electrodes for fuel cells, Zn-air batteries, and water splitting.

Ruoqiu Du received his Bachelor's degree in 2018 from Beijing University of Chemical Technology (BUCT). He joined Professor Feng Wang's group as a master candidate at BUCT in 2018. His research interests include the synthesis, functionalization of carbon nanomaterials for energy-related applications.

Yihuan Yu received his Bachelor's degree in 2015 from Beijing University of Chemical Technology (BUCT). He joined Professor Feng Wang's group as a Ph.D. candidate at BUCT in 2015. His research interests include the design, preparation and performance evaluation of single-atom electrocatalysts for energy storage and conversion.

Zhengping Zhang is an associate professor in Beijing University of Chemical Technology (BUCT). Dr. Zhang gained his Ph.D. in BUCT in 2017, respectively. He accepted a Joint training doctoral fellowship in 2016, and became a visiting fellow in Case Western Reserve University (CWRU) for one year. Dr. Zhang research interests include the synthesis, functionalization, and theoretical calculation of metal-organic macrocyclic compounds and carbon nanomaterials for energy-related applications.

Feng Wang joined Beijing University of Chemical Technology (BUCT) as the full professor in the College of Materials Science and Engineering in 2006. He is also the director of Beijing Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Process and Technology for Materials. Prof. Wang received his Ph.D. in Tokyo Metropolitan University in 2003. His expertise covers electro-catalytic materials, nanocarbon materials, and applied electrochemical engineering.
